question: A process instance's token reaches a parallel gateway with three outgoing sequence flows. According to the BPMN primer, what happens at the gateway?

options.text: The gateway chooses one of the three outgoing sequence flows based on process data, the same way an exclusive gateway does.
The parallel gateway generates new tokens by activating all three outgoing sequence flows in parallel.
The token waits at the gateway until all three downstream activities have already completed; only then does it pass.
The token is consumed and only the first downstream activity that registers a job worker is executed.

a.text: Choosing one outgoing flow based on data is the behavior of an exclusive gateway, not a parallel gateway. Parallel gateways do not evaluate data conditions for routing.

b.text: The primer states that the parallel gateway generates new tokens by activating multiple sequence flows in parallel. Each outgoing flow advances independently from that point.

c.text: Parallel gateways do not wait for downstream completion before forwarding the token; the join behavior on the receiving side is what waits, not the splitting side.

d.text: There is no behavior in which the gateway picks the first worker to register. All outgoing flows are activated; each one independently triggers its downstream elements.

explanation: Gateways route tokens in more complex patterns than plain sequence flow. The primer contrasts two gateway behaviors: an exclusive gateway chooses one outgoing sequence flow based on data, while a parallel gateway generates new tokens by activating multiple outgoing sequence flows in parallel. A three-way parallel split therefore produces three concurrent tokens.
